era_context: The Roman Empire in the early Third Century Crisis. Emperor Decius (249–251 CE) was succeeded by Trebonianus Gallus (251–253 CE). The Plague of Cyprian was at its height. Gothic invasions threatened the Danube frontier. The Sassanid Empire under Shapur I was consolidating power. In China, the Wei-Jin period continued with competing kingdoms.

events.title: Battle of Abrittus: Death of Emperor Decius
events.description: Emperor Decius was defeated and killed in battle against the Goths at Abrittus (modern Razgrad, Bulgaria) in 251 CE. This was a catastrophic defeat for the Roman Empire and marked the first time a reigning Roman emperor died in battle against a foreign enemy. The battle resulted in the loss of Roman control over parts of Moesia and demonstrated the vulnerability of the Roman military during the Third Century Crisis. Decius' death led to the succession of Trebonianus Gallus.
events.certainty_note: Decius' death at Abrittus in 251 CE is well-attested in multiple sources, though details of the battle are sparse and sometimes contradictory.
cross_references: Led to Trebonianus Gallus' succession (251–253 CE)

events.title: Trebonianus Gallus Becomes Roman Emperor
events.description: Following the death of Decius at Abrittus, Trebonianus Gallus became Roman Emperor. Gallus was a senator and military commander who was proclaimed emperor by the troops. His reign was marked by continued plague, barbarian invasions, and civil instability. Gallus made peace with the Goths, agreeing to pay tribute in exchange for their withdrawal from Roman territory—a controversial decision that some sources criticized as weakness.
events.certainty_note: Trebonianus Gallus' accession in 251 CE is well-attested, though details of his early reign are sparse.

events.title: Plague of Cyprian at Peak Mortality
events.description: The Plague of Cyprian, which began around 249–250 CE, reached peak mortality in 251 CE. Cyprian of Carthage documented severe mortality in North Africa, describing mass deaths, social breakdown, and the Christian community's response through charitable care. The plague caused significant economic disruption, military recruitment difficulties, and social instability. The exact mortality rate is unknown, but contemporary accounts suggest it was catastrophic in urban centers.

sources.name: Cyprian of Carthage, De Mortalitate (On Mortality) and correspondence

events.certainty_note: The plague's existence and impact are documented, but precise dating of peak mortality and mortality figures are uncertain. Some scholars place peak mortality in 250–251 CE, others in 252–253 CE.
cross_references: Part of the Plague of Cyprian (249–262 CE)

events.title: Persecution of Christians under Decius (Aftermath)
events.description: The persecution of Christians initiated by Emperor Decius (249–251 CE) continued into 251 CE and beyond. Decius had ordered all inhabitants of the empire to perform pagan sacrifices; those who refused were executed or imprisoned. The persecution was particularly severe in North Africa and Egypt. Following Decius' death at Abrittus, the persecution continued under Trebonianus Gallus, though with less intensity. Cyprian of Carthage was among those who fled persecution.

sources.name: Cyprian of Carthage, correspondence and De Lapsis (On the Lapsed)

events.certainty_note: The persecution of Christians under Decius is well-attested in Christian sources, though the extent and intensity are debated.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Battle of Abrittus is sometimes given as 250 CE or 251 CE in different sources. Some modern scholars question whether Decius actually died at Abrittus or in a subsequent battle. The Historia Augusta is unreliable for details of this period. The Plague of Cyprian's identity and extent remain debated; some scholars argue for bubonic plague, others for smallpox or measles. The extent of Decius' persecution of Christians is debated; some sources may exaggerate its severity.
historiographic_note: 251 CE is moderately well-documented compared to other years in the Third Century Crisis, primarily because of the dramatic death of an emperor in battle and the detailed accounts of Christian persecution. However, the Historia Augusta remains unreliable, and later sources like Zosimus are not contemporary. Cyprian of Carthage provides valuable primary evidence for the plague and persecution, but his perspective is limited to North Africa and is shaped by Christian concerns. Archaeological evidence (coins, inscriptions, settlement patterns) is crucial but unevenly distributed. Non-Mediterranean regions are almost entirely absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Decius' death at Abrittus led to Trebonianus Gallus' succession
Plague of Cyprian continued into subsequent years
Persecution of Christians was initiated by Decius in 249 CE
